examples.title: OS Structure - System Call Interface Simulation
examples.description: Demonstrates the basic structure of an operating system by simulating the user space and kernel space interaction through system calls
examples.code:
import os
import sys

# Simulating OS Structure
# User Space Applications

def user_application():
    """User level program requesting OS services"""
    print("User Process: Requesting file open operation")
    result = os_system_call("open", "file.txt", "r")
    print(f"User Process: Received result - {result}")
    
    print("User Process: Requesting memory allocation")
    result = os_system_call("allocate", 1024)
    print(f"User Process: Memory allocated at {result}")

# Kernel Space (OS Core)
def os_system_call(syscall_name, *args):
    """Kernel function handling system calls"""
    # Kernel mode - privileged operations
    if syscall_name == "open":
        return kernel_file_open(args[0], args[1])
    elif syscall_name == "allocate":
        return kernel_memory_allocate(args[0])
    return None

def kernel_file_open(filename, mode):
    """Kernel level file handler"""
    print(f"  [KERNEL] Checking file permissions for {filename}")
    print(f"  [KERNEL] Allocating file descriptor")
    return f"fd:{(hash(filename) % 100)}"

def kernel_memory_allocate(size):
    """Kernel level memory manager"""
    print(f"  [KERNEL] Allocating {size} bytes from heap")
    print(f"  [KERNEL] Updating page table")
    return hex(id(size))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    print("=== OS Structure: User-Kernel Interaction ===")
    user_application()
examples.explanation: This example illustrates the two-level structure of modern operating systems: 1) User Space where applications run with limited privileges, 2) Kernel Space where privileged OS operations occur. The os_system_call() function represents the system call interface - the boundary between user and kernel mode. When user applications need services like file access or memory allocation, they make system calls which the kernel handles with elevated privileges.

examples.title: Process Management - PCB and Process States
examples.description: Demonstrates OS process management by simulating Process Control Block (PCB) and process state transitions
examples.code:
from enum import Enum
from collections import deque
import time
import random

class ProcessState(Enum):
    NEW = "New"
    READY = "Ready"
    RUNNING = "Running"
    WAITING = "Waiting"
    TERMINATED = "Terminated"

class PCB:
    """Process Control Block - Data structure holding process information"""
    def __init__(self, pid, name):
        self.pid = pid
        self.name = name
        self.state = ProcessState.NEW
        self.program_counter = 0
        self.registers = [0] * 5
        self.memory_limits = (0, 1024)
        self.creation_time = time.time()

    def __repr__(self):
        return f"PCB(PID:{self.pid}, {self.name}, {self.state.value})"

class ProcessManager:
    """OS Process Management Component"""
    def __init__(self):
        self.ready_queue = deque()
        self.waiting_queue = deque()
        self.running_process = None
        self.pcb_table = {}
        self.pid_counter = 1

    def create_process(self, name):
        """OS Operation: Create new process"""
        pcb = PCB(self.pid_counter, name)
        self.pcb_table[pcb.pid] = pcb
        self.ready_queue.append(pcb)
        pcb.state = ProcessState.READY
        self.pid_counter += 1
        print(f"[OS] Created process: {pcb.name} (PID: {pcb.pid})")
        return pcb

    def schedule(self):
        """OS Operation: Process Scheduling (FCFS)"""
        if not self.ready_queue and not self.running_process:
            print("[OS] No processes to schedule")
            return

        # If no running process, pick from ready queue
        if not self.running_process and self.ready_queue:
            self.running_process = self.ready_queue.popleft()
            self.running_process.state = ProcessState.RUNNING
            print(f"[OS] Scheduled: {self.running_process.name} -> RUNNING")

    def context_switch(self):
        """OS Operation: Context Switch"""
        if self.running_process:
            # Save current process state
            pcb = self.running_process
            print(f"[OS] Context Switch: Saving state for PID {pcb.pid}")
            
            # Move to ready or terminated
            if random.random() > 0.3:  # 70% chance process continues
                pcb.state = ProcessState.READY
                self.ready_queue.append(pcb)
            else:
                pcb.state = ProcessState.TERMINATED
                print(f"[OS] Process {pcb.name} TERMINATED")
            
            self.running_process = None
            
        # Schedule next process
        self.schedule()

    def simulate_io_wait(self):
        """OS Operation: Process moves to Waiting state for I/O"""
        if self.running_process:
            self.running_process.state = ProcessState.WAITING
            self.waiting_queue.append(self.running_process)
            self.running_process = None
            print(f"[OS] {self.waiting_queue[-1].name} moved to WAITING (I/O)")
            self.schedule()

    def io_completion(self):
        """OS Operation: I/O complete, move to Ready queue"""
        if self.waiting_queue:
            pcb = self.waiting_queue.popleft()
            pcb.state = ProcessState.READY
            self.ready_queue.append(pcb)
            print(f"[OS] I/O complete: {pcb.name} -> READY")

# Demonstration
print("=== OS Process Management Demo ===\n")
os_sim = ProcessManager()

# Create processes
os_sim.create_process("Chrome")
os_sim.create_process("VSCode")
os_sim.create_process("Spotify")

print("\n--- Scheduling ---")
os_sim.schedule()

print("\n--- Context Switch 1 ---")
os_sim.context_switch()

print("\n--- Simulate I/O Wait ---")
os_sim.simulate_io_wait()

print("\n--- I/O Completion ---")
os_sim.io_completion()

print("\n--- Final Context Switch ---")
os_sim.context_switch()

print(f"\n[OS] Ready Queue: {list(os_sim.ready_queue)}")
print(f"[OS] Waiting Queue: {list(os_sim.waiting_queue)}")
examples.explanation: This example demonstrates core OS process management operations: 1) PCB (Process Control Block) - the data structure the OS uses to maintain information about each process, 2) Process States - NEW, READY, RUNNING, WAITING, TERMINATED, 3) Ready Queue - holds processes waiting for CPU, 4) Context Switch - saving and restoring process state, 5) Scheduling - deciding which process runs next. The ProcessManager class simulates how an OS manages multiple processes competing for CPU time.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for queue operations, O(1) for PCB lookup
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is number of processes

examples.title: Memory Management - Paging and Page Replacement
examples.description: Demonstrates OS memory management concepts including virtual memory, page tables, and page replacement algorithms (FIFO and LRU)
examples.code:
from collections import OrderedDict
from collections import deque

class MemoryManagementUnit:
    """Simulates OS Memory Management with Paging"""
    
    def __init__(self, num_frames=4):
        self.num_frames = num_frames
        self.frames = [None] * num_frames  # Physical memory frames
        self.page_table = {}  # Virtual page -> Frame mapping
        self.page_faults = 0
        self.page_hits = 0
        
    def reset(self):
        self.frames = [None] * self.num_frames
        self.page_table = {}
        self.page_faults = 0
        self.page_hits = 0

class FIFOPageReplacement(MemoryManagementUnit):
    """First In First Out Page Replacement Algorithm"""
    
    def __init__(self, num_frames=4):
        super().__init__(num_frames)
        self.queue = deque()  # Track page insertion order

    def access_page(self, page_number):
        """Access a virtual page"""
        if page_number in self.page_table:
            # Page hit - already in memory
            self.page_hits += 1
            print(f"  [HIT] Page {page_number} already in Frame {self.page_table[page_number]}")
        else:
            # Page fault - need to load page
            self.page_faults += 1
            print(f"  [FAULT] Page {page_number} not in memory")
            
            # Check if there's free frame
            if None in self.frames:
                frame_index = self.frames.index(None)
            else:
                # Replace oldest page (FIFO)
                oldest_page = self.queue.popleft()
                frame_index = self.page_table[oldest_page]
                del self.page_table[oldest_page]
                print(f"  [REPLACE] Evicting Page {oldest_page} from Frame {frame_index}")
            
            # Load new page
            self.frames[frame_index] = page_number
            self.page_table[page_number] = frame_index
            self.queue.append(page_number)
            print(f"  [LOAD] Page {page_number} loaded into Frame {frame_index}")

    def display_state(self):
        print(f"\n  Frames: {self.frames}")
        print(f"  Page Faults: {self.page_faults}, Hits: {self.page_hits}")
        print(f"  Hit Ratio: {self.page_hits / (self.page_faults + self.page_hits):.2%}")

class LRUPageReplacement(MemoryManagementUnit):
    """Least Recently Used Page Replacement Algorithm"""
    
    def __init__(self, num_frames=4):
        super().__init__(num_frames)
        self.access_order = OrderedDict()  # Track LRU order

    def access_page(self, page_number):
        """Access a virtual page using LRU"""
        if page_number in self.page_table:
            self.page_hits += 1
            # Update LRU order
            self.access_order.move_to_end(page_number)
            print(f"  [HIT] Page {page_number} in Frame {self.page_table[page_number]}")
        else:
            self.page_faults += 1
            print(f"  [FAULT] Page {page_number} not in memory")
            
            # Check for free frame
            if None in self.frames:
                frame_index = self.frames.index(None)
            else:
                # Replace least recently used
                lru_page = next(iter(self.access_order))
                frame_index = self.page_table[lru_page]
                del self.page_table[lru_page]
                del self.access_order[lru_page]
                print(f"  [REPLACE] Evicting Page {lru_page} (LRU) from Frame {frame_index}")
            
            self.frames[frame_index] = page_number
            self.page_table[page_number] = frame_index
            self.access_order[page_number] = True
            print(f"  [LOAD] Page {page_number} loaded into Frame {frame_index}")

    def display_state(self):
        print(f"\n  Frames: {self.frames}")
        print(f"  Page Faults: {self.page_faults}, Hits: {self.page_hits}")
        print(f"  Hit Ratio: {self.page_hits / (self.page_faults + self.page_hits):.2%}")

# Demonstration
print("=== OS Memory Management: Paging Demo ===\n")

# Page reference sequence
page_sequence = [1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

print("Page Reference Sequence:", page_sequence)
print(f"Number of Physical Frames: 4\n")

# FIFO Algorithm
print("--- FIFO Page Replacement ---")
fifo = FIFOPageReplacement(num_frames=4)
for page in page_sequence:
    print(f"Accessing Page {page}:")
    fifo.access_page(page)
fifo.display_state()

# Reset for LRU
fifo.reset()

print("\n--- LRU Page Replacement ---")
lru = LRUPageReplacement(num_frames=4)
for page in page_sequence:
    print(f"Accessing Page {page}:")
    lru.access_page(page)
lru.display_state()

print("\n=== Concepts Demonstrated ===")
print("1. Virtual Memory: Programs see larger address space than physical memory")
print("2. Page Table: Maps virtual pages to physical frames")
print("3. Page Fault: Occurs when accessed page not in memory")
print("4. Page Replacement: Evicting pages when memory is full")
print("5. FIFO: Evicts oldest page; LRU: Evicts least recently used")
examples.explanation: This advanced example demonstrates critical OS memory management operations: 1) Paging - dividing virtual memory into fixed-size pages and physical memory into frames, 2) Page Table - maintains mapping between virtual and physical addresses, 3) Page Faults - when a requested page is not in physical memory, 4) Page Replacement Algorithms - FIFO (First In First Out) evicts the oldest page, LRU (Least Recently Used) evicts the least recently accessed page. The simulation shows how modern operating systems implement virtual memory to give processes the illusion of having contiguous memory while physical memory is fragmented and shared.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of page accesses, O(1) per page access
examples.spaceComplexity: O(f) where f is number of frames
